subject: Cardiology
title: Heart failure
preambleMarkdown:
# Heart failure

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is an examination-focused draft. Local protocols, medication choices, procedure thresholds, anticoagulation decisions, pregnancy/pediatric care, and device or surgical indications must be checked against current institutional and national guidance before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Heart failure involves impaired ventricular filling, ejection, or myocardial performance causing congestion, low output, or both. Exam questions emphasize volume status, ejection fraction phenotype, precipitating causes, guideline-directed medical therapy, device indications, and acute decompensation sequencing.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Classify by EF and by congestion/perfusion profile.
- HFrEF disease-modifying therapy includes ARNI/ACEi/ARB, evidence beta blocker, MRA, and SGLT2 inhibitor when tolerated.
- Diuretics treat congestion.
- Search for ischemia, valve disease, arrhythmia, toxins, and infiltrative disease.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Heart failure is a clinical syndrome of symptoms and signs caused by structural or functional cardiac abnormality, supported by objective evidence such as natriuretic peptides or imaging.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- HFrEF, HFmrEF, HFpEF, and improved EF.
- Acute decompensated versus chronic compensated disease.
- Left-sided, right-sided, or biventricular failure.
- Ischemic, hypertensive, valvular, genetic, infiltrative, inflammatory, toxic, tachycardia-mediated, or stress-related causes.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Heart failure is common in older adults and patients with hypertension, coronary disease, diabetes, kidney disease, obesity, valve disease, cardiomyopathy, atrial fibrillation, cancer therapy exposure, or prior myocarditis. Exams emphasize phenotype, congestion, perfusion, and reversible triggers.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Key anatomy includes ventricular size and thickness, atrial size, valves, pericardium, coronary supply, pulmonary vasculature, and systemic venous drainage. Chamber geometry helps distinguish dilated, hypertrophic, restrictive, ischemic, valvular, and right-sided patterns.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Symptoms arise when filling pressures rise, forward output falls, or both. Neurohormonal activation initially preserves perfusion but later promotes sodium retention, vasoconstriction, remodeling, congestion, and arrhythmia risk.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Heart failure can result from systolic dysfunction, diastolic dysfunction, pressure overload, volume overload, ischemic scar, infiltrative disease, myocarditis, tachycardia-mediated dysfunction, toxin exposure, or stress cardiomyopathy. Decompensation often reflects infection, ischemia, arrhythmia, renal failure, medication nonadherence, or excess salt/fluid.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Major causes include ischemic heart disease, hypertension, valve disease, genetic cardiomyopathy, alcohol or stimulant exposure, anthracycline or other cardiotoxic therapy, myocarditis, infiltrative disease, endocrine disease, tachyarrhythmia, congenital disease, and peripartum cardiomyopathy.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess hypertension, coronary disease, diabetes, chronic kidney disease, obesity, sleep apnea, alcohol, cocaine or amphetamines, cardiotoxic chemotherapy, family cardiomyopathy, atrial fibrillation, valve disease, and prior myocarditis.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Genetic contribution is most important when presentation is early, familial, syndromic, associated with sudden death, or linked to cardiomyopathy, channelopathy, aortopathy, or congenital heart disease. First-degree relatives may need screening when an inherited syndrome is suspected.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Microbiology is usually not the primary driver unless infection causes endocarditis, myocarditis, pericarditis, infected aneurysm, sepsis-related cardiovascular collapse, prosthetic-device infection, or vascular graft infection. Fever, bacteremia, immunosuppression, injection drug use, or recent procedures should prompt an infectious evaluation.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Dyspnea, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, edema, fatigue, weight gain, rales, elevated JVP, S3, hepatomegaly, or cool extremities.
- Low-output states may present with confusion, renal dysfunction, hypotension, or narrow pulse pressure.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Ask about onset, duration, triggers, exertional component, positional or pleuritic features, syncope, dyspnea, palpitations, chest pain, neurologic symptoms, bleeding, fever, medication use, stimulant use, pregnancy, recent procedures, prior ECG or imaging, prior cardiovascular disease, and family history of sudden death or inherited disease.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess vital signs, oxygenation, mental status, perfusion, volume status, pulses, jugular venous pressure, precordial impulse, heart sounds, murmurs, lungs, edema, neurologic deficits, skin findings, and signs of shock. Compare bilateral pulses and blood pressures when aortic or vascular disease is possible.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Hypotension, altered mental status, cold extremities, or oliguria.
- Severe hypoxemia or pulmonary edema.
- New chest pain or ischemic ECG changes.
- Syncope or sustained ventricular arrhythmia.
- Rapidly progressive cardiomyopathy.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Dyspnea and edema alternatives include COPD or asthma, pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, renal failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, venous insufficiency, anemia, thyroid disease, obesity hypoventilation, and medication-related edema.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use ECG, chest imaging, natriuretic peptides, renal function, electrolytes, CBC, thyroid testing when indicated, and echocardiography.
- Evaluate ischemia and valve disease when clinically appropriate.
- Use cardiac MRI for myocarditis, infiltrative disease, scar, or uncertain cardiomyopathy.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Use ECG, chest imaging, natriuretic peptides, CBC, renal function, electrolytes, liver tests when congested, thyroid testing when indicated, troponin when ischemia is possible, echocardiography for EF and structure, ischemia evaluation, cardiac MRI for tissue characterization, and selected invasive hemodynamics.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Diagnosis should combine symptoms, examination, ECG or rhythm evidence, imaging, biomarkers, and accepted disease-specific criteria. Avoid diagnosing from one abnormal test in isolation when the clinical syndrome does not fit.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Severity is based on hemodynamic stability, symptom burden, organ dysfunction, ECG or imaging risk features, biomarker pattern, ventricular function, lesion severity, arrhythmia risk, embolic risk, bleeding risk, frailty, and need for urgent procedural intervention.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Initial Management

- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure. Place unstable patients on monitoring, obtain IV access, treat hypoxemia, correct immediately reversible causes, obtain an ECG, and escalate early to cardiology, emergency medicine, ICU, vascular surgery, electrophysiology, or cardiac surgery when high-risk features are present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, smoking cessation, exercise or cardiac rehabilitation when appropriate, diet quality, weight management, sleep apnea treatment, alcohol or stimulant reduction, vaccination when relevant, and shared decision-making. Activity restrictions depend on acuity, symptoms, rhythm risk, structural disease, and procedure plans.

### Pharmacological Management

- Medication choices depend on the exact syndrome and contraindications. Common classes include antiplatelets, anticoagulants, statins, antihypertensives, beta blockers, calcium-channel blockers, nitrates, diuretics, guideline-directed heart-failure therapies, antiarrhythmics, antibiotics, or analgesics. Dose details require local protocol and patient-specific renal, hepatic, pregnancy, bleeding, and interaction review.

### Procedures

- Procedural options may include cardioversion, defibrillation, temporary or permanent pacing, catheter ablation, coronary angiography or PCI, CABG, valve intervention, vascular repair, thrombectomy, thrombolysis, pericardiocentesis, endovascular repair, or diagnostic catheterization depending on the topic.

### Surgical Management

- Surgery or structural intervention is considered for unstable anatomy, refractory symptoms despite medical therapy, high-risk coronary or valvular disease, congenital lesions, aortic disease, vascular complications, device infection, or complications such as mechanical rupture. Timing depends on acuity, anatomy, procedural risk, expected durability, and patient goals.

- Acute congestion: oxygen or ventilation support when needed, IV loop diuretics, vasodilators if appropriate, and trigger treatment.
- Shock: inotropes, vasopressors, mechanical circulatory support, and ICU consultation when indicated.
- Chronic care: guideline-directed therapy, sodium and fluid counseling when relevant, rehabilitation, device therapy, and close follow-up.
- Avoid medications that worsen HF when possible.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Monitor symptoms, vital signs, rhythm, perfusion, urine output when ill, medication adverse effects, renal function and electrolytes when using diuretics or renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system therapy, anticoagulation safety, device function when present, and disease-specific imaging intervals.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Follow-up should confirm diagnosis, review response to therapy, reconcile medications, address risk factors, arrange rehabilitation or specialist care, document warning symptoms, and ensure surveillance for recurrence, progression, procedural complications, or adverse drug effects.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Complications may include shock, heart failure, pulmonary edema, myocardial infarction, stroke, systemic embolism, sudden cardiac death, malignant arrhythmia, bleeding from therapy, renal injury, infection, recurrent hospitalization, procedure-related injury, and chronic functional limitation.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prognosis depends on acuity, ventricular function, comorbid disease, age, kidney function, adherence, response to therapy, reversibility of the cause, and access to timely emergency or procedural care. Early recognition and appropriate follow-up reduce preventable morbidity.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prevention includes smoking cessation, blood-pressure control, lipid management, diabetes care, physical activity, diet quality, weight management, treatment of sleep apnea, avoidance of cardiotoxic exposures, vaccination when relevant, and family screening for inherited disease when indicated.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Explain the diagnosis, uncertainty that remains, warning symptoms, medication purpose and adverse effects, follow-up plan, activity guidance, and when to seek urgent care. Use plain language and confirm understanding.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Trend vital signs, oxygenation, pain, mental status, perfusion, rhythm, intake/output when ill, and response to treatment.
- Escalate promptly for red flags, new neurologic symptoms, recurrent chest pain, hypotension, syncope, worsening dyspnea, or malignant rhythm.
- Reinforce medication adherence, follow-up, lifestyle changes, and procedure instructions.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy status when relevant, interactions, duplicate anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy, QT-prolonging drugs, and adherence barriers.
- High-risk medications require indication, duration, monitoring plan, and bleeding or toxicity counseling.
- Reconcile medications at transitions of care.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Most cardiology conditions do not automatically require antibiotic prophylaxis for dental care. Consider endocarditis prophylaxis only for guideline-defined high-risk cardiac conditions and procedures, and coordinate anticoagulant management for invasive dental procedures when needed.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Pediatrics

- Pediatric presentations may reflect congenital disease, inherited arrhythmia, myocarditis, Kawasaki disease, multisystem inflammatory syndromes, or rare familial vascular disease. Use age-specific vital signs, doses, and pediatric specialist pathways.

### Pregnancy

- Pregnancy changes heart rate, plasma volume, coagulation, and imaging or medication choices. Maternal stabilization comes first in emergencies. Coordinate cardiology, obstetrics, anesthesia, and neonatology for significant disease.

### Older Adults

- Older adults often have atypical symptoms, polypharmacy, frailty, kidney disease, cognitive impairment, and higher bleeding or procedural risk. Goals of care and functional status should shape decisions.

### Renal Impairment

- Renal disease changes biomarker interpretation, medication dosing, contrast risk, electrolyte vulnerability, and bleeding risk. Avoid delaying life-saving imaging or intervention when the emergency risk is greater than contrast risk.

### Hepatic Impairment

- Assess coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, drug metabolism, volume status, and procedural bleeding risk. Medication selection and anticoagulation decisions require individualized review.

### Immunocompromised Patients

- Maintain a lower threshold for infection, atypical presentations, drug interactions, and device-related complications. Coordinate antimicrobial and procedural decisions with specialists when infection is possible.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Major North American and European guidance generally agrees on stabilizing emergencies first, using ECG and echocardiography appropriately, treating modifiable risk factors aggressively, and matching procedures to anatomy and risk. Differences commonly involve thresholds, timing, scoring systems, medication preferences, and local resource pathways. Use current local guidance for patient care.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stability determines the first action.
- Compare current ECG, imaging, and symptoms with prior data whenever possible.
- Treat the patient, not a single number.
- Reassess after each intervention.
- Imported notes below are provenance; current synthesis should be reviewed against up-to-date guidelines.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Delaying emergency treatment for nonessential testing.
- Ignoring medication contraindications, renal function, pregnancy, or bleeding risk.
- Assuming a normal initial test excludes a dynamic cardiovascular process.
- Failing to arrange follow-up after an ED or inpatient diagnosis.
- Removing imported source notes instead of preserving provenance.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### USMLE Step 1

- Focus on anatomy, physiology, pathology, pharmacology, ECG mechanisms, hemodynamics, and classic disease associations.

### USMLE Step 2 CK

- Prioritize presentation, next best diagnostic test, initial stabilization, red flags, and first-line management.

### USMLE Step 3

- Emphasize sequencing: stabilize first, choose the right unit of care, monitor treatment response, prevent complications, and plan discharge follow-up.

### ABIM

- Expect guideline nuance, comorbid disease, renal impairment, anticoagulation decisions, procedural indications, and treatment failure.

### ABFM

- Focus on prevention, screening when applicable, risk-factor control, medication adherence, counseling, and recognizing emergencies that need transfer.

### Surgery boards

- Know when vascular, cardiac, or structural intervention is urgent; understand anatomy, perioperative risk, and complications.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- BNP/NT-proBNP support diagnosis but are context dependent.
- Echo is essential.
- Do not start beta blocker in cardiogenic shock.
- CRT depends on EF, QRS duration/morphology, rhythm, and symptoms.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Areas requiring ongoing review include individualized thresholds for intervention, risk prediction in underrepresented groups, long-term comparative effectiveness of procedures and devices, optimal antithrombotic duration, and implementation of guideline-directed therapy in patients with multimorbidity.

sections.bodyMarkdown:

### Source Excerpt: Congestive Heart Failure

_Source lines: 1140-1775._

```text
Congestive Heart Failure
A. General Characteristics

1. CHF is a clinical syndrome resulting from the heart's inability to
meet the body's circulatory demands under normal physiologic
conditions. It is the final common pathway for a wide variety of
cardiac diseases (see also Clinical Pearl 1-5)

## 2. Pathophysiology (Figure 1-6)

a. Frank-Starling relationship
- In a normal heart, increasing preload results in greater
contractility
- When preload is low (at rest), there is little difference in
performance between a normal and a failing heart. However,
with exertion a failing heart produces relatively less
contractility and symptoms occur (Figure 1-7)

## 3. Heart Failure with reduced Ejection Fraction (HFrEF) or systolic

dysfunction
a. Owing to impaired contractility (reduced EF <40%)
b. Causes include:
- Ischemic heart disease or after a recent MI-infarcted
cardiac muscle does not pump blood (decreased EF)
- Idiopathic
- HTN
- Myocarditis (postviral, giant cell, autoimmune)
- Drugs: Alcohol, cocaine, methamphetamines, chemotherapy
(anthracyclines and trastuzumab)
- Infiltrative disease (amyloidosis, sarcoidosis,
hemochromatosis, Wilson disease)
- Radiation therapy
- Thyroid disease
- Peripartum cardiomyopathy
- Infectious disease (Chagas disease, HIV, endocarditis causing
valvular disease)
- Valvular heart disease (usually MR, aortic stenosis [AS] or
regurgitation)
- High-output heart failure (severe anemia, due to AV fistulas,
pregnancy, severe thiamine deficiency)
- Congenital/hereditary
CLINICAL PEARL 1-5
High-Output Heart Failure
1. In high-output heart failure, an increase in cardiac output is needed for
the requirements of peripheral tissues for oxygen.

2. Causes include:
- Chronic anemia
- Pregnancy
- Hyperthyroidism
- AV fistulas
- Wet beriberi (caused by thiamine [vitamin B 1] deficiency)
- Paget disease of bone
- MR
- Aortic insuficiency
11. The conditions listed above rarely cause heart failure by themselves.

However, if these conditions develop in the presence of underlying heart
disease, heart failure can result quickly.

FIGURE
1-6 Pathophysiology of CHF.

FIGURE
1-7 Frank-Starling relationship.

## 1. Heart Failure with preserved Ejection Fraction (HFpEF) or diastolic

dysfunction
a. Owing to impaired ventricular filling during diastole (either
impaired relaxation or increased stiffness of ventricle or both)
b. Echocardiogram shows impaired relaxation of left ventricle but
EF >50%
c. Causes include:
- HTN leading to myocardial hypertrophy-most common
cause of diastolic dysfunction
- Valvular diseases such as AS, mitral stenosis, and aortic
regurgitation
- Restrictive cardiomyopathy (e.g., amyloidosis, sarcoidosis,
hemochromatosis in their early phases)

## 2. Heart Failure with mid-range Ejection Fraction (HFmrEF)

a. Newer terminology for patients with an EF between 40% and
50%
b. Pathophysiology and outcomes typically on the spectrum
between HFrEF and HFpEF
B. Clinical Features
1. Symptoms of left-sided heart failure (see also Clinical Pearl 1-6)
a. Dyspnea-dificulty breathing secondary to pulmonary
congestion/edema

b. Orthopnea-dificulty breathing in the recumbent position;
relieved by elevation of the head with pillows
c. Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND)-awakening after 1 to 2
hours of sleep due to acute shortness of breath (SOB)
d. Nocturnal cough (nonproductive)-worse in recumbent position
(same pathophysiology as orthopnea)
e. Confusion and memory impairment occur in advanced CHF as a
result of inadequate brain perfusion
f. Diaphoresis and cool extremities at rest-occur in desperately ill
patients (NYHA class IV)
CLINICAL PEARL 1-6
New York Heart Association (NYHA) Classification
- NYHA class I: Symptoms only occur with vigorous activities, such as
playing a sport. Patients are nearly asymptomatic.

- NYHA class II: Symptoms occur with prolonged or moderate exertion,
such as climbing a flight of stairs or carrying heavy packages. Slight
limitation of activities.

- NYHA class III: Symptoms occur with usual activities of daily living, such
as walking across the room or getting dressed. Markedly limiting.

- NYHA class IV: Symptoms occur at rest. Incapacitating.

1. Signs of left-sided heart failure
a. Displaced PMI (usually to the left) due to cardiomegaly
b. Pathologic S 3 (ventricular gallop)
- Rapid filling phase "into" a noncompliant left ventricular
chamber
- May be normal finding in children; in adults, usually
associated with CHF
- May be dificult to hear, but is among the most specific signs
of CHF
- Heard best at apex with bell of stethoscope
- The sequence in the cardiac cycle for S 3: S 3 follows S 2 (ken-
tuck-Y)
h. S4 gallop
- Sound of atrial systole as blood is ejected into a
noncompliant, or stiff, left ventricular chamber
- Heard best at left sternal border with bell of stethoscope
- The sequence in the cardiac cycle for S 4: S 4 precedes S 1
(TEN-nes-see)

l. Crackles/rales at lung bases
- Caused by fluid spilling into alveoli; indicates pulmonary
edema
- Rales heard over lung bases suggest at least moderate
severity of left ventricular heart failure
o. Dullness to percussion and decreased tactile fremitus of lower
lung fields caused by pleural effusion
p. Increased intensity of pulmonic component of second heart
sound suggests pulmonary HTN (heard over left upper sternal
border)
2. Symptoms/signs of right-sided heart failure
a. Peripheral pitting edema-pedal edema lacks specificity as an
isolated finding. In the elderly, it is more likely to be secondary
to venous insuficiency
b. Nocturia-due to increased venous return with elevation of legs
c. Jugular venous distention (JVD)
d. Hepatomegaly/hepatojugular reflux
e. Ascites
f. Right ventricular heave (found with pulmonary HTN)
3. Given enough time, left-sided heart failure can lead to right-sided
heart failure (most common cause of right-sided heart failure)
a. Patients often present with sign/symptoms of both right- and
left-sided HF
Quick HIT
Tests to order for a new patient with CHF:
- CBC (anemia)
- BMP (assess for renal injury due to volume overload, or
cardiorenal syndrome)
- Cardiac enzymes to rule out MI
- BNP
- CXR (pulmonary edema, cardiomegaly, rule out COPD)
- ECG
- Echocardiogram
C. Diagnosis

## 1. CXR (Figure 1-8)

a. Cardiomegaly
b. Kerley B lines are short horizontal lines near periphery of the
lung near the costophrenic angles, and indicate pulmonary
congestion secondary to dilation of pulmonary lymphatic
vessels

c. Prominent interstitial markings
d. Pleural effusion
e. Lung ultrasound can also be used to evaluate for pulmonary
edema and pleural effusion
2. Echocardiogram (transthoracic)
a. Initial test of choice-should be performed whenever CHF is
suspected based on history, examination, or CXR
b. Useful in determining whether systolic or diastolic dysfunction
is present, and may help to identify the underlying cause of
heart failure
c. Shows chamber dilation and/or hypertrophy
d. Evaluates valvular disease
3. B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) is released from the ventricles in
response to ventricular volume expansion and pressure overload
a. BNP levels >100 pg/mL correlate strongly with the presence of
decompensated CHF (unit of measurement varies)
b. BNP may be useful in differentiating between dyspnea caused
by CHF and COPD.

c. N-terminal pro-BNP (NT-proBNP) is a newer assay with similar
predictive value as BNP. The normal range for this value
depends on the age of the patient, but an NT-proBNP <300
virtually excludes the diagnosis of HF
d. BNP may be falsely low in obese patients
4. ECG is usually nonspecific but can be useful for detecting chamber
enlargement and presence of ischemic heart disease or prior MI
5. Radionuclide ventriculography using technetium-99m ("nuclear
ventriculography"). Also called multigated acquisition (MUGA)
scan
6. Cardiac catheterization can provide valuable quantitative
information regarding diastolic and systolic dysfunction, and can
clarify the cause of CHF if noninvasive test results are equivocal.

Consider coronary angiography to exclude CAD as an underlying
cause of CHF
7. Stress testing
a. Identifies ischemia and/or infarction
b. Quantifies level of conditioning
c. Assesses dynamic responses of HR, heart rhythm, and BP

FIGURE
1-8 A: CXR showing cardiogenic pulmonary edema. B: Another example of
cardiogenic pulmonary edema; note cardiomegaly (patient had CHF).

(A reprinted with permission from Mergo PJ. Imaging of the Chest-A Teaching File.

Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2002:50. Figure 24A. B reprinted with permission from Stern
EJ, White CS. Chest Radiology Companion. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 1999:38. Figure
5-4.)

Quick HIT
HTN is a common cause of CHF and should be treated. Goal is to
reduce preload and afterload.

Quick HIT
The RALES trial showed that spironolactone reduces morbidity and
mortality in patients with class III or IV heart failure with EF <35%. It
is contraindicated in renal failure (creatinine >2).

Quick HIT
Monitoring a patient with CHF:
- Weight-unexplained weight gain can be an early sign of
worsening CHF
- Clinical manifestations (exercise tolerance is key); peripheral
edema
- Laboratory values (electrolytes, K, BUN, creatinine levels;
serum digoxin level, if applicable)
D. Chronic Treatment

## 1. HFrEF

a. General lifestyle modification
- Sodium restriction (less than 4 g/day)
- Fluid restriction (1.5 to 2.0 L daily)
- Weight loss
- Smoking cessation
- Restrict alcohol use
- Exercise program
- All patients should monitor weight daily to detect fluid
accumulation
- Annual influenza vaccine and pneumococcal vaccine
recommended
j. Diuretics
- Most effective means of providing symptomatic relief to
patients with moderate-to-severe CHF.

- Recommended for patients with systolic failure and volume
overload.

- Have not been shown to reduce mortality or improve
prognosis, just for symptom control. Goal is relief of signs
and symptoms of volume overload (dyspnea, peripheral
edema).

- Loop diuretics are first line and include furosemide,
bumetanide, and torsemide. Furosemide has very
inconsistent oral bioavailability compared to bumetanide
and torsemide.

- Thiazide-like diuretics: metolazone and chlorothiazide. Used
to augment diuresis with loop diuretics.

p. Therapies to improve outcomes: Standard therapy includes the
following (medications in parentheses have been shown to
improve mortality in HFrEF):
- First-line therapies include ACEi/ARB, ARNI, beta-blockers, and
hydralazine with a nitrate for those who do not tolerate
ACEi/ARB/ARNI
- ACEi (enalapril, lisinopril, captopril) or ARB (valsartan,
losartan, candesartan)
- beta-Blockers (metoprolol succinate, carvedilol, bisoprolol)
- Primary prevention of sudden cardiac death with ICD
implantation if EF <35% on optimal medical therapy
- Hydralazine and nitrates (isosorbide dinitrate) are an
effective combination to reduce afterload in patients with
HFrEF who do not tolerate an ACEi/ARB/ARNI. Prior
studies showed the greatest benefit in Black patients
- Sacubitril/valsartan is an angiotensin receptor/neprilysin
inhibitor (ARNI) that can be used in patients with EF

<35% with maximally tolerated ACEi or ARB, and has
been shown to improve mortality (PARADIGM-HF trial)
- Second-line therapies include mineralocorticoid receptor
antagonists (MRAs), SGLT2 inhibitors, ivabradine,
vericiguat, and digoxin
- MRAs (spironolactone, eplerenone), if EF <35%
- SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin) reduced mortality in the
DAPA-HF trial, in HFrEF patients WITH or WITHOUT
type 2 DM
- Ivabradine is a funny channel inhibitor that has been
shown to reduce hospitalizations in patients with
reduced EF and HR >70 on maximally tolerated beta-
blocker therapy
- Vericiguat is an oral soluble guanylate cyclase simulator
that causes vasodilation. It was studied in the VICTORIA
trial, which showed a reduction in the composite
outcome of death from a cardiovascular cause and first
HF hospitalization (though both individual outcomes
were nonsignificant)
- Digoxin was once regularly used for HFrEF, but has not
been shown to improve mortality. It works as a positive
inotropic agent that can provide short-term symptomatic
relief. Serum levels should be monitored periodically.

Signs of digoxin toxicity include GI (nausea, vomiting,
anorexia), cardiac (ectopy, AV block), CNS (visual
disturbances, confusion)
Quick HIT
ACE Inhibitors
- Cause venous and arterial dilation, decreasing preload and
afterload and thus blood pressure.

- ACEis reduce mortality (Cooperative North Scandinavian
Enalapril Survival Study [CONSENSUS] and Studies of Left
Ventricular Dysfunction [SOLVD] trials), prolong survival, and
alleviate symptoms in mild, moderate, and severe HFrEF.

- Monitor BP, potassium, and creatinine.

Quick HIT
Most common cause of death from HF is sudden death from
ventricular arrhythmias. Ischemia provokes ventricular arrhythmias.

Quick HIT
Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers (ARBs):
- Can be used in patients unable to take ACEis due to side effect
of cough
beta-Blockers (specifically metoprolol succinate, carvedilol, and
bisoprolol have been shown to reduce mortality):
- Proven to decrease mortality in patients with HFrEF.

- Reported to improve symptoms of HF; may slow progression of
heart failure by slowing down tissue remodeling. The decrease
in heart rate leads to decreased oxygen consumption.

- beta-Blockers have antiarrhythmic, anti-ischemic, and antianginal
effect.

Quick HIT
Aldosterone Antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone)
- Indicated for HFrEF with EF <35%
- Improve all-cause mortality, CV mortality, and hospitalizations
- Can cause hyperkalemia, hence BMP should be monitored (do
not start if creatinine >2.5 in men or 2 in women)
A 62-year-old man with a history of hypertension and a myocardial
infarction 2 years ago is hospitalized for acute decompensated heart
failure. He is diuresed with improvement in symptoms and is discharged
on appropriate medications. He is seen in clinic 4 months later with
complaints of worsening symptoms over the last month. Previously he
became mildly short of breath after significant exertion, but he now
reports severe shortness of breath after walking only 50 m. He is
asymptomatic only at rest. His current medications include aspirin,
sacubitril-valsartan, carvedilol, furosemide, atorvastatin, omega-3 fatty
acids, and pantoprazole. An ECG performed in the ofice shows Q waves
in leads V3 and V4, with a normal QRS duration. An echocardiogram
performed 1 week ago showed an ejection fraction of 30%.

The patient has been encouraged to quit smoking and drinking alcohol,
and to eat a low-sodium diet. What additional therapy is recommended
at this time?

A. Lisinopril
B. Spironolactone
C. Cardiac resynchronization therapy
D. Hydralazine and isosorbide dinitrate
E. Both B and C

F. None of the above
- The answer is B: Spironolactone. The patient displays New York
Heart Association (NYHA) class III symptoms (no symptoms at rest,
but symptoms with minimal exertion). He is on an appropriate initial
regimen, which includes a loop diuretic, an angiotensin-neprilysin
inhibitor (ARNI), and a beta-blocker. The three beta-blockers shown to
have a survival benefit in heart failure are carvedilol, metoprolol,
and bisoprolol. Additional pharmacologic treatment is indicated and
includes an aldosterone antagonist (e.g., spironolactone) and a
sodium-glucose co-transporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitor. Notable side
effects of spironolactone include hyperkalemia, gynecomastia, and
agranulocytosis. The patient should also consider an ICD given his
history of myocardial infarction and reduced ejection fraction. ICDs
have been shown to reduce mortality, both for primary prevention
and for secondary prevention of fatal arrhythmias. (A) The patient is
already on an ARNI, which includes an ARB, and there is no benefit
to adding an ACE inhibitor. (C) Cardiac resynchronization therapy
(CRT) may be considered in heart failure patients with dyssynchrony
between the left and right ventricles, and therefore criteria for CRT
include a QRS duration >120 ms. This patient does not meet
criteria. (D) Hydralazine and nitrates are used as alternatives to ACE
inhibitors and ARBs if the patient cannot tolerate one of these agents.

1. HFpEF: Few therapeutic options available; patients are treated
symptomatically (NO medications have proven mortality benefit)
- Diuretics are used for symptom control (volume overload)
- Patients should continue salt and fluid restriction, take daily
weights
- No other drug classes have been shown to produce significant
mortality benefit in randomized controlled trials
5. General principles in treatment of HF (also see Clinical Pearl 1-7)
a. The following medications should be used with caution in
patients with CHF:
- Metformin-may cause potentially lethal lactic acidosis with
renal dysfunction
- Thiazolidinediones-causes fluid retention
- NSAIDs may increase risk of CHF exacerbation
- Some antiarrhythmic agents that have negative inotropic
effects
- CCBs may increase mortality
- Drugs that can cause hyperkalemia or renal injury
- Cardiotoxic drugs, such as some chemotherapy drugs
i. The following devices have been shown to reduce mortality in
selected patients:

- An ICD lowers mortality by helping prevent sudden cardiac
death (which is the most common cause of death in CHF). It
is indicated for patients at least 40 days post-MI, EF <35%,
and class II or III symptoms despite optimal medical
treatment
- Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT): This is
biventricular pacemaker-indications are similar to ICD
except these patients also have prolonged QRS duration
>130 ms. Most patients who meet criteria for CRT are also
candidates for ICD and receive a combined device
Cardiac transplantation is the last alternative if the above do not
control symptoms.

CLINICAL PEARL 1-7
Ventricular Assist Devices in Heart Failure
- Ventricular assist device (VAD). Work on these devices has been
occurring for the past five decades, and most of the products on the
market now represent research that was done 30 years ago. There is
great potential for these devices as more research is conducted and
clinicians become more experienced in their advantages and drawbacks.

- VADs may be used to support the left ventricle (LVAD), right ventricle
(RVAD), or both ventricles (BiVAD).

- The pump is implanted in the abdominal cavity with cannulation to the
heart. The system controller and battery are worn externally.

- These devices may allow for a patient requiring continuous hospitalization
to eventually be discharged with relatively straightforward outpatient
follow-up. Patients may live with these devices for a year or more.

- Lifelong anticoagulation with heparin or warfarin is essential without
exceptions as these devices are very thrombogenic.

- VADs may also be used for hemodynamic support related to the acute
treatment of a STEMI or other cardiac pathology (i.e., while recovering
from a severe case of myocarditis).

Quick HIT
The overall 5-year mortality for all patients with CHF is about 50%.

Acute Decompensated Heart Failure
A. Findings

1. Acute dyspnea associated with elevated left-sided filling pressures,
with or without pulmonary edema.

2. Most commonly due to LV systolic or diastolic dysfunction.

3. Flash pulmonary edema refers to a severe form of heart failure with
rapid accumulation of fluid in the lungs.

B. Diagnosis
1. Differential includes PE, asthma, and pneumonia, all of which can
cause rapid respiratory distress.

2. Diagnostic tests include ECG, CXR, ABG, BNP, echocardiogram, and
possible coronary angiogram if new onset heart failure and high
suspicion for ischemic disease.

C. Treatment
1. Oxygenation and ventilatory assistance with nonrebreather face
mask, noninvasive positive pressure ventilation, or even intubation
as indicated.

2. Diuretics to treat volume overload and congestive symptoms-this
is the most important intervention. Decreases preload.

3. Vasodilators (nitroprusside, nitrates) can be considered in specific
patients, such as those with severe HTN or mitral regurgitation.

4. Patients who have pulmonary edema despite use of oxygen,
diuretics, and nitrates may benefit from use of inotropic agents
(dobutamine). Digoxin takes several weeks to work and is not
indicated in an acute setting.

5. Dietary sodium and fluid restriction.

6. Mechanical support devices can be considered in patients with
shock.

Arrhythmias
Terminology
- Arrhythmias are typically categorized as whether the QRS complex is
narrow or wide (>120 ms).

- Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) is a broad term that typically
refers to tachyarrhythmias with a source above the ventricles (SA
node, atria, AV node, His bundle, or a combination of these). SVTs are
typically narrow complex, though they can also be wide complex
(e.g., SVT with aberrancy).

- The term paroxysmal SVT (PSVT) is often used to describe a subset of
SVT that starts and stops abruptly, and are regular. These typically

include atrial tachycardia, AV nodal reentrant tachycardia (AVNRT),
AV reentrant/reciprocating tachycardia (AVRT).

Premature Complexes
A. Premature Atrial Complexes
1. This early beat arises within the atria, firing on its own.

2. Causes include adrenergic excess, drugs, alcohol, tobacco,
electrolyte imbalances, ischemia, hypoxia, and infection.

3. On ECG, look for early P waves that differ in morphology from the
normal sinus P wave (because these P waves originate within the
atria and not the sinus node).

4. QRS complex is normal because conduction below the atria is
normal. There is usually a pause before the next sinus P wave.

5. Premature atrial complexes (PACs) are found in more than 50% of
normal adults who undergo Holter monitoring and are of no
significance in a normal heart, but may be a precursor of ischemia
in a diseased heart.

6. May cause palpitations or give rise to SVT.

7. Usually asymptomatic and do not require treatment. Monitor for
increased frequency. If symptomatic (e.g., palpitations), beta-blockers
may be helpful.

Quick HIT
The Cardiac Arrhythmia Suppression Trial (CAST) I and CAST II
studies showed that the use of antiarrhythmic drugs to suppress
PVCs after MI increases the risk of death.

Quick HIT
PVCs
- Couplet: Two successive PVCs
- Bigeminy: Sinus beat followed by a PVC
- Trigeminy: Two sinus beats followed by a PVC
B. Premature Ventricular Complexes
1. This early beat fires on its own from a focus in the ventricle and
then spreads to the other ventricle.

2. PVCs can occur in patients with or without structural heart disease.

Causes include hypoxia, electrolyte abnormalities, stimulants,
caffeine, medications, and structural heart disease.

3. Since conduction is not through normal conduction pathways, but
rather through ventricular muscle, it is slower than normal, causing
a wide QRS.

4. Wide, bizarre QRS complexes followed by a compensatory pause
are seen; a P wave is not usually seen because it is "buried" within
the wide QRS complex.

5. PVCs appear in more than 50% of men who undergo 24-hour
Holter monitoring.

6. Most patients are asymptomatic. Some patients may have
palpitations and dizziness related to PVCs. If symptomatic, beta-
blockers may be used.

7. Presence of PVCs in patients with normal hearts is associated with
increased mortality.

8. If a patient is found to have frequent PVCs, workup for underlying
structural heart disease should be initiated which may require
specific treatment.

9. Patients with frequent, repetitive PVCs and underlying heart
disease are at increased risk for sudden death due to cardiac
arrhythmia (especially VFib). Order an electrophysiologic study
because patients may benefit from an ICD or ablation.

Quick HIT
Patients with AFib in the presence of underlying heart disease have
an especially high risk of embolization and hemodynamic
compromise.

Tachyarrhythmias
```

<!-- source: lib.md import end -->

- AHA/ACC/HFSA. 2022 Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-management-of-heart-failure
- ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS. 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation. https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001193
- ACC/AHA. 2020 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Valvular Heart Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2020-acc-aha-guideline-for-the-management-of-patients-with-valvular-heart-disease
- ACC/AHA. 2022 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-diagnosis-and-management-of-aortic-disease
- ACC/AHA. 2024 Guideline for the Management of Lower Extremity Peripheral Artery Disease. https://professional.heart.org/-/media/PHD-Files-2/Science-News/2/2024/2024-PAD-guideline-slide-set.pdf
